mnemonics.title: ACID Properties
mnemonics.content: A transaction is ACID: Atomic (all or nothing), Consistent (valid state), Isolated (concurrent appears serial), Durable (persists after crash). Think: 'Always Consume Ice Daily' - Atomic Consistent Isolated Durable

mnemonics.title: Transaction States Flow
mnemonics.content: Active → Partially Committed → Committed (success path); Active/Failed → Aborted (failure path). Mnemonic: 'Act Partially Commit Fail Abort' - A-P-C-F-A

mnemonics.title: Isolation Level Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: From lowest to highest: RU (Read Uncommitted) → RC (Read Committed) → RR (Repeatable Read) → S (Serializable). Mnemonic: 'U Can Rarely See' - Uncommitted, Committed, Repeatable, Serializable

acronyms.usage: Four fundamental properties that guarantee reliable database transactions
Standard language for managing relational databases and transactions
SQL commands like INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE that modify data and participate in transactions
Commands like CREATE, ALTER that auto-commit and cannot be rolled back in most databases
SQL statements: COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT that manage transactions

visualTips.visualization: Draw a state diagram: Active (start) → arrows to Partially Committed (execute) → Committed (success); or to Failed (error) → Aborted (rollback). Circle states, arrow transitions with labels.
Picture a protective bubble around each transaction - Atomic (invisible boundary), Consistent (checks at entry/exit), Isolated (separate lanes), Durable (iron vault).
Create a 2x2 table: S+S = Compatible, S+X = No, X+S = No, X+X = No. Remember: Only Shared+Shared can coexist.
Draw a staircase from bottom (RU - most concurrency, least safety) to top (S - least concurrency, most safety). Show which problems each step prevents.

analogies.analogy: Like cash transfer: withdraw from one account (debit) AND deposit to another (credit). Either both happen (committed) or neither happens (rolled back). ATM doesn't give cash if deposit fails.
Just as a plane trip is either complete or doesn't happen, a transaction either fully completes or has no effect. No partial flights, no partial transactions.
Each transaction works in its own private room, unaware of others. Results appear as if only one transaction runs at a time, even when multiple run concurrently.
Once committed, data is like a signed contract - it survives system crashes, power failures, or restarts. Cannot be undone once properly signed and stored.
